clinical_coherence.notes: 45F with 12-year RRMS on natalizumab presenting with new bilateral leg weakness that she attributes to MS relapse. Brilliant case design: OLD stable MS findings (optic pallor, brisk reflexes, reduced vibration) are meticulously documented and clearly distinguished from NEW functional signs (Hoover, give-way weakness, tubular visual fields, midline splitting, gait inconsistency). Psychosocial precipitants (marital conflict, disability application, fear of SPMS) are realistic. The dual pathology concept is clinically authentic.

tool_output_consistency.notes: MRI shows stable MS lesions with ZERO new or enhancing lesions, perfectly concordant with the non-relapse diagnosis. EEG normal. Labs show positive JCV antibody (relevant for PML risk on natalizumab, not for current symptoms). VEPs and SSEPs show OLD stable abnormalities only. Comparison MRI confirms absolute stability. All outputs concordant.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD F44.4 correct. Critical action to NOT escalate MS immunotherapy is essential. Differential includes MS relapse, SPMS, and depression-related psychomotor slowing. Contraindicated actions include IV methylprednisolone and DMT escalation.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Diagnostic puzzle perfectly calibrated. The known MS diagnosis creates an extremely strong cognitive anchor. Distinguishing new functional signs from old stable MS deficits requires expertise. This is the ultimate dual-pathology challenge.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 45F with RRMS on natalizumab, occupational therapist on disability, marital conflict over disability insurance. All highly realistic. Medications appropriate (natalizumab, duloxetine, naproxen).

red_herring_quality.notes: Three excellent red herrings: (1) Known MS creating reflexive relapse attribution, (2) brisk reflexes as seemingly active UMN signs (actually old stable), (3) fear of SPMS transition reinforcing the MS progression narrative. Each is realistic and clinically challenging.

educational_value.notes: Exceptional teaching case. FND-MS comorbidity is a real and under-recognized clinical problem (up to 30% of MS patients). Teaches the critical skill of distinguishing new functional signs from old neurological deficits, the importance of stable MRI in excluding relapse, and the need for transparent dual-diagnosis communication.

strengths: Masterfully designed dual-pathology case (FND superimposed on MS)
Meticulous documentation of OLD vs NEW findings throughout
Stable MRI comparison as the key discriminating investigation
Realistic psychosocial precipitants in the context of chronic neurological disease
VEP and SSEP follow-ups showing only old stable abnormalities
Health anxiety and illness beliefs assessment in psychiatric evaluation
